claims.text: In 1979 the US Army launched Project Jedi, a program to build an army of psychic soldiers that became known as the First Earth Battalion.

evidence.quote:
In 1979, the US
Army launched Project Jedi, a program to
build an army of psychic soldiers.

They
were called the First Earth Battalion.

claims.rationale: The First Earth Battalion Operations Manual (Jim Channon, 1979) is a real declassified document. 'Project Jedi' as an umbrella label is informal journalism; the Army's official psychic programs cycle through names (SCANATE, GONDOLA WISH, GRILL FLAME, CENTER LANE, SUN STREAK, STAR GATE).

claims.text: At Fort Bragg, Army researchers tried to kill goats using only their minds at a classified facility called the Goat Lab.

evidence.quote:
They're trying to kill goats with
their minds.

claims.rationale: The Goat Lab at Fort Bragg existed (Special Forces medical training used live goats), and Jon Ronson's 'The Men Who Stare at Goats' documents Guy Savelli's claim to have stopped a goat's heart. Whether the experiment actually worked is another question (see c_0013).

claims.text: Lt. Col. Jim Channon spent 319 days in combat in Vietnam and his platoon suffered zero casualties after the first day and never killed an innocent civilian.

evidence.quote: Shannon spent 319 days in combat.

his platoon suffered zero casualties,
and they never killed a single innocent
civilian.

claims.rationale: Jim Channon's Vietnam service and 173rd Airborne record are established; the specific 'zero casualties for 317 days' and 'never killed a civilian' figures come from Channon's own recollections and his First Earth Battalion lore, which historians regard as self-mythologized.

claims.text: Channon wrote the First Earth Battalion manual at Fort Leavenworth in 1979; it is a real declassified document available online.

evidence.quote:
Shannon wrote it at Fort Levvenworth in
1979.

It's been
declassified and archived online.

claims.rationale: The First Earth Battalion Operations Manual is indeed available online (archive.org, and Channon's own website). Leavenworth authorship is verified via Army bio.

claims.text: Major General Albert Stubblebine III, commander of Army Intelligence and Security Command, repeatedly tried to walk through walls in the Pentagon and hit his head.

evidence.quote:
Stubble believed he
could walk through walls.

He hit his head hard again and
again and again.

This man commanded Army
intelligence during the height of the
Cold War.

claims.rationale: Stubblebine's wall-walking anecdote is repeatedly confirmed in his own interviews (Jon Ronson, History Channel). It is one of the few psychic-warfare stories with direct on-record corroboration from the participant.

claims.text: Under Stubblebine, the Army brought in celebrity psychic Uri Geller to test whether he could telepathically erase computer discs or trigger nuclear launch codes.

evidence.quote:
They brought in psychic Yuri
Geller.

claims.rationale: Uri Geller was engaged by SRI and the CIA/Army in the 1970s — confirmed in declassified STARGATE files. Geller himself has long described Stubblebine-era testing.

claims.text: CIA remote viewers produced drawings matching Soviet facilities they had never seen, including a shipyard sketch that matched satellite photos three days later.

evidence.quote:
Remote viewers
described a massive Soviet submarine
under construction

His
drawing matched satellite photos taken 3
days later.

claims.rationale: Some STARGATE sessions did produce suggestive sketches (notably Pat Price's Semipalatinsk session), but the 'three days later satellite confirmation' is a best-case cherry-pick; overall program accuracy when blindly graded was at chance.

claims.text: Ingo Swann claimed to remotely view Jupiter's rings before NASA discovered them.

evidence.quote:
Ingo Swan, another
legendary viewer, claimed he could see
Jupiter's rings before NASA discovered
them.

claims.rationale: The Swann-Jupiter session (1973) did include a ring-like feature, but his description also contained many misses, and skeptics note Jupiter rings were anticipated in astronomy before Pioneer 10 confirmed them.

claims.text: The CIA officially shut down its psychic spy programs in 1995.

evidence.quote:
The CIA officially shut down its psychic
spy programs in 1995

claims.rationale: The CIA STARGATE program was officially terminated in 1995 following the AIR report. This is fully declassified public record.

claims.text: The 1989 Panama invasion used Channon's psychotronic sound-weapon concept — surrounding Noriega's compound with speakers blasting Van Halen and The Clash for three days until he surrendered.

evidence.quote:
They didn't shoot.
They set up massive speakers and played
Van Halen and the Clash at deafening
volumes for three days straight.

He
surrendered.

claims.rationale: Rock-music siege of the Vatican embassy in Panama City (Dec 1989) is well-documented, but Noriega surrendered primarily for diplomatic reasons (papal nuncio pressure). The music's causal role is disputed.

claims.text: The Air Force Wright Laboratory proposed a 'gay bomb' — an aphrodisiac chemical weapon to make enemy troops sexually attracted to each other — as a real funded proposal in 1994.

evidence.quote:
In 1994, the Air Force Right Laboratory
proposed a halattosis bomb

Then came the gay
bomb.

It was a real
funded proposal.

claims.rationale: The Wright Lab 'Harassing, Annoying and Bad Guy Identifying Chemicals' proposal (1994, FOIA-released 2005) is a matter of documented record. It was never developed into a deployed weapon.

claims.text: Guy Savelli successfully stopped goat number 17's heart with his mind during a Goat Lab experiment.

evidence.quote:
he stopped
the goat's heart with his mind.

he was aiming for goat
16, but accidentally killed goat number
17.

claims.rationale: The story is self-reported by Savelli, uncontrolled and unreplicated. The 'wrong goat' admission strongly suggests coincidence, not psychic kinetics. The host explicitly flags this as a tell.

claims.text: The CIA's own review concluded that remote viewing never produced reliable results and was indistinguishable from good guessing.

evidence.quote:
the CIA said it
never produced reliable results.

claims.rationale: The 1995 American Institutes for Research (AIR) evaluation commissioned by the CIA concluded remote viewing had no operational utility. This is the standard skeptical endpoint for STARGATE.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] AIR review undercut the cherry-picked hits
[debunks] same review applies to Swann's sessions

claims.text: Channon's Vietnam platoon outcomes are explained by high situational awareness and training, not psychic ability.

evidence.quote:
his soldiers weren't psychic. They were
just really well trained.

claims.rationale: The host explicitly adopts this steelman: Channon's results are real but attributable to mindfulness and training, not ESP. Neuroscience supports the claim that meditation improves threat detection.

claims.text: Col. John Alexander, a former First Earth Battalion member, became known as 'the father of non-lethal weaponry' — showing Channon's work quietly survived inside mainstream Army doctrine.

evidence.quote:
Colonel John Alexander, a former member
of the first earth battalion, became
known as the father of non-lethal
weaponry.

claims.rationale: Col. John Alexander is a real figure — author of 'The Warrior's Edge' and 'Future War'; his role developing Army non-lethal weapons programs at Los Alamos is well documented.
